courses.name: General Chemistry I
textbooks: Chemistry, Atoms First, 2nd Ed.; Steven Zumdahl, Cengage Publishing
Chemistry (on-line text); Paul Flowers, William Robinson, et.al.; OpenStax Publishing
courses.description: Chemistry 102 is the first semester course in introductory chemistry. The course covers the following topics: Principles governing atomic structure, bonding, states of matter, stoichiometry, and chemical equilibrium.
objectives: How does the atomic structure of particles give rise to periodic trends
How/why do atoms come together to form molecules in a variety of different ways bonding and structure
How do molecules exist in three dimensions molecular shape
How do gas particles interact with each other and the surroundings gasses and kinetic molecular theory
What causes gasses to condense into other forms of matter intermolecular forces
Why do reactions take place with defined ratios of reactants stoichiometry
why do some reactions 'work' and others don’t thermodynamics
why does a reaction stop when it does equilibrium and thermodynamics
To apply the lessons we’ve learned in previous chemistry classes as well as the material wewill learn in this class to relevant real-world applications.
To develop critical thinking and problem solving skills that will be useful in this course as well as other courses that you will take throughout your college career.
We will do this by working through problems and experiments in class, the lab, and perhaps most importantly, onour own outside of class.
To gain an appreciation for the chemistry that is everywhere in the world around us (even if it still isn’t your favorite subject).

d_entry: The action or an act of measuring or calculating a length, quantity, value, etc.

To count the number of significant figures in a given number, start with the first non-zero digit from the left and, moving to the right, count all the digits thereafter, counting final zeros if they are to the right of the decimal point. For example, 1.2048, 1.2040, 0.012048, 0.0012040 and 1204.0 all have 5 significant figures. In rounding or truncation of a number to n significant figures, the original is replaced by a number with n significant figures. Note that final zeros to the left of the decimal point may or may not be significant: the number 1,204,000 has at least 4 significant figures, but without more information there is no way of knowing whether or not any more figures are significant. When 1,203,960 is rounded to 5 significant figures to give 1,204,000, an explanation that this has 5 significant figures is required. This could be made clear by writing it in scientific notation: 1.2040 × 10^6. To say that a = 1.2048 to 5 significant figures means that 1.20475 ≤ a < 1.20485.

The units used for measuring physical quantities in the internationally agreed system Système International d’Unités are known as SI units. There are seven base units and other supplementary units and prefixes (such as kilo-) to create further units.

A theory of chemical combination first stated by John Dalton in 1803. It involves the following postulates: 1) elements consist of indivisible small particles. 2) All atoms of the same element are identical; different elements have different types of atoms. 3) Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed. 4) compound elements or compounds are formed when atoms of different elements join in simple ratios to form molecules

The smallest part of an element than can exist chemically. Atoms consist of a small dense nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by moving electrons. The number of electrons equals the number of protons so the overall charge is zero.
The number of nucleons in an atomic nucleus of a particular nuclide
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. The atomic number is equal to the number of electrons orbiting the nucleus in a neutral atom.
One of two or more atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons in their nucleus but different number of neutrons.
A table of elements arranged in order of increasing proton number to show the similarities of chemical elements with related electronic configuration.

A unit of mass used to express the relative atomic masses. It is equal to 1/12 of the mass of an atom of the isotope carbon-12 and is equal to 1.66033 x 10^-27 kg. This unit superseded both the physical and chemical mass units based on oxygen-16 and is sometimes called the unified mass unit or the dalton.

The SI unit of amount of substance. It is equal to the amount of substance that contains as many elementary units as there are atoms in 0.012 kg of carbon-12. The elementary units may be atoms, molecules, ions, radicals, electrons, etc., and must be specified. 1 mole of a compound has a mass equal to its relative molar mass expressed in grams.
The number of atoms or molecules in one mole of substance. It has the value of 6.22140857 x 10^23.

Energy resulting from the acceleration of electric charge and the associated electric fields and magnetic fields. The energy can be regarded as waves propagated through space (requiring no supporting medium) involving oscillating electric and magnetic fields at right angles to each other and to the direction of propagation.

In a vacuum the waves travel with a constant speed (the speed of light) of 2.9979 x 10^8 metres per second; if material is present they are slower. Alternatively, the energy can be regarded as a stream of photons traveling at the speed of light, each photon having an energy hc/lambda, where h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, and lambda is the wavelength of the associated wave. A fusion of these apparently conflicting concepts is possible using the methods of quantum mechanics. The characteristics of the radiation depend on its wavelength.

The liberation of electrons from a substance exposed to electromagnetic radiation. The number of such electrons (photoelectrons) emitted depends on the frequency of the radiation. The effect is a quantum process in which the radiation is regarded as a stream of photons, each having an energy, hf, where h is the Planck constant and f is the frequency of the radiation. A photon can only eject an electron if the photon energy exceeds the work function, phi, of the solid, i.e. if hf0 = pho an electron will be ejected; f0 is the minimum frequency (or threshold frequency) at which ejection will occur. For many solids the photoelectric effect occurs at ultraviolet frequencies or above, but for some materials (having low work functions) it occurs with light.
A colourless odourless gaseous chemical element atomic number 1. It is the lightest and the most abundant in the universe. It is present in water and all organic compounds.
Niels Bohr proposed a model of the atom with a central nucleus surrounded by orbiting electrons.

A system of mechanics that was developed from quantum theory and is used to explain the properties of atoms and molecules. Using the energy quantum as a starting point it incorporates Heisenberg's uncertainty principle and the DeBroglie wavelength to establish teh wave-particle duality on which the Schrodinger equation is based.

The principle quantum number, n, gives the main energy level and has values 1, 2, 3, etc. (the higher the number, the further on average the electron from the nucleus). Traditionally, these levels, or the orbits corresponding to them, are referred to as shells and given letters K, L, M, etc. The K-shell is the one nearest the nucleus. The maximum number of electrons in a given shell is 2n^2.

A region in which an electron may be found in an atom or molecule. In the original Bohr theory of the atom the electrons were assumed to move around the nucleus in circular orbits, but further advances in quantum mechanics led to the view that it is not possible to give a definite path for an electron. According to wave mechanics the electron has a certain probability of being in a given element of space. Thus for a hydrogen atom the electron can be anywhere from close to the nucleus to out in space but the maximum probability in spherical shells of equal thickness occurs in a spherical shell around the nucleus with a radius equal to the Bohr radius of the atom. The probabilities of finding an electron in different regions can be obtained by solving the Schrodinger wave equation to give the wave function 11.), and the probability of location per unit volume is then proportional to 41 2. Thus the idea of electrons in fixed orbits has been replaced by that of a probability distribution around the nucleus- an atomic orbital. Alternatively, the orbital can be thought of as an electric charge distribution (averaged over time). In representing orbitals it is convenient to take a surface enclosing the space in which the electron is likely to be found with a high probability.
The arrangement of electrons about the nucleus of an atom.

The elements fall into vertical columns, known as groups. Going down a group, the atoms of the elements all have the same outer shell structure, but an increasing number of inner shells. Traditionally, the alkali metals were shown on the left of the table and the groups were numbered IA to VILA, IB to VIM, and 0 (for the noble gases). All the elements in the middle of the table are classified as 'transition elements and the nontransition elements are regarded as main-group elements. Because of confusion in the past regarding the numbering of groups and the designations of subgroups, modern practice is to number the groups across the table from 1 to 18. Horizontal rows in the table are periods. The first three are called short periods; the next four (which include transition elements) are long periods. Within a period, the atoms of all the elements have the same number of shells, but with a steadily increasing number of electrons in the outer shell. The periodic table can also be divided into four blocks depending on the type of shell being filled: the 's-block, the 'p-block the 'd-block and the y-block. There are certain general features of chemical behaviour shown in the periodic table. In moving down a group, there is an increase in metallic character because of the increased size of the atom. In going across a period, there is a change from metallic (electropositive) behaviour to nonmetallic (electronegative) because of the increasing number of electrons in the outer shell. Consequently, metallic elements tend to be those on the left and towards the bottom of the table; nonmetallic elements are towards the top and the right. There is also a significant difference between the elements of the second short period (lithium to fluorine) and the other elements in their respective groups. This is because the atoms in the second period are smaller and their valence electrons are shielded by a small 1s2 inner shell. Atoms in the other periods have inner s- and p-electrons shielding the outer electrons from the nucleus. Moreover, those in the second period only have s- and p-orbitals available for bonding. Heavier atoms can also promote electrons to vacant d-orbitals in their outer shell and use these for bonding.

A representation of the structure of a molecule in the form of a diagram showing the bonds between atoms; dots are used to represent valence electrons, with a shared pair of dots between atoms representing a covalent bond. These diagrams were devised by Gilbert Newton Lewis in 1916. Although this representation of chemical bonds was replaced by quantum chemistry, Lewis structures have remained useful, albeit in a limited way.

The rule stating that many atoms tend to form molecules such that the outermost shell of the atom has eight electrons, thus giving the atom the same electron configuration as the noble gases. This rule was postulated by Gilbert Newton Lewis in 1916 and was the first electron counting rule in chemistry. There are many exceptions to the octet rule.

The representation of the structure of a molecule by two or more conventional formulae. For example, the formula of methanal can be represented by a covalent structure H2C=O, in which there is a double bond in the carbonyl group. It is known that in such compounds the oxygen has some negative charge and the carbon some positive charge. The true bonding in the molecule is somewhere between H2C=O and the ionic compound H2C+O-.

A property of some elementary particles that gives rise to an interaction between them and consequently to the host of material phenomena described as electrical. Charge occurs in nature in two forms, conventionally described as positive and negative in order to distinguish between the two kinds of interactions between particles. Two particles that have similar charges (both negative or both positive) interact by repelling each other; two particles that have dissimilar charges (one positive, one negative) interact by attracting each other.
A theory for predicting the shapes of molecules. It postulates that the shapes of molecules are mostly determined by the repulsions between pairs of valence electrons, including lone pairs.
The branch of chemistry concerned with compounds of carbon.

A molecule that has a dipole moment; i.e. one in which there is some separation of charge in the chemical bonds, so that one part of the molecule has a positive charge and the other a negative charge.

In considering the formation of molecular orbitals it is often useful to think in terms of hybrid atomic orbitals. For instance, carbon has in its outer shell one s-orbital and three p-orbitals. In forming methane (or other tetrahedral molecules) these can be regarded as combining to give four equivalent spa hybrid orbitals, each with a lobe directed to a corner of a tetrahedron. It is these that overlap with the s-orbitals on the hydrogen atoms. In ethene, two p-orbitals combine with the s-orbital to give three sp2 hybrids with lobes in a plane pointing to the corners of an equilateral triangle. These form the sigma orbitals in the C-H and C-C bonds. The remaining p-orbitals (one on each carbon) form the pi orbital. In ethyne, sp hybridization occurs to give two hybrid orbitals on each atom with lobes pointing along the axis. The two remaining p-orbitals on each carbon form two pi orbitals. Hybrid atomic orbitals can also involve d-orbitals. For instance, square-planar complexes use sped hybrids; octahedral complexes use sp3d2. Orbital hybridization can be discussed using group theory.
Describing chemical reactions in which the reactants combine in simple whole-number ratios.
The mass concentration (symbol rho) is the mass solute per unit volume of solution.

A way of representing a chemical compound using symbols for the atoms present. The molecular formula simply gives the types and numbers of atoms present. For example, the molecular formula of ethanoic acid is C2H4O2. The empirical formula gives the atoms in their simplest ratio; for ethanoic acid it is CH2O.
A way of denoting a chemical reaction using the symbols for the participating particles (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.).

A chemical reaction in which a substance reacts rapidly with oxygen with the production of heat and light. Such reactions are often free-radical chain reactions, which can usually be summarized as the oxidation of carbon to form its oxides and teh oxidation of hydrogen to form water.
The actual amount of the product that is formed divided by the maximum possible amount of a product that can be formed from a given amount of reactant in a chemical reaction, times 100 percent.

A liquid that conducts electricity as a result of the presence of positive or negative ions. Electrolytes are molten ionic compounds or solutions containing ions, i.e. solutions of ionic salts or of compounds that ionize in solution. Liquid metals, in which the conduction is by free electrons, are not usually regarded as electrolytes. Solid conductors of ions, as in the sodium-sulphur cell, are known as electrolytes.
The amount of substance dissolved per unit volume of solution (symbol c) has units of mol dm-3 or mol per liter. It is now called amount concentration (formerly molarity).
The formation of a small solid particulate produced in a liquid by chemical reaction.

A change in which one or more compounds (the reactants) form new compounds (the products). All reactions are to some extent reversible; i.e. the products can also react to give the original reactants. However, in many cases the extent of this back reaction is negligibly small, and the reaction is regarded as irreversible.

Originally, oxidation was simply regarded as a chemical reaction with oxygen. The reverse process - loss of oxygen - was called reduction. Reaction with hydrogen also came to be regarded as reduction. Later, a more general idea of oxidation and reduction was developed in which oxidation was loss of electrons and reduction was gain of electrons. This wider definition covered the original one.
A state of matter in which the matter concerned occupies the whole of its container irrespective of its quantity.
An equation that relates the pressure, p, the volume, V, and thermodynamic temperature, T, of an amount of substance, n.

The simplest equation of state, pV=nRT, where R is the universal gas constant. Applying only to ideal gases, this equation takes no account of the volume occupied by the gas molecules (according to this law if the pressure is infinitely great the volume becomes zero), nor does it take into account any forces between molecules.
Weak forces occurring between molecules.

The pressure exerted by a vapour. All solids and liquids give off vapours, consisting of atoms or molecules of the substances that have evaporated from the condensed forms. These atoms or molecules will exert a vapour pressure.
A graph showing the relationship between solid, liquid, and gaseous phases over a range of conditions (e.g. temperature and pressure).
The branch of physical chemistry concerned with the heats of chemical reaction, heats of formation of chemical compounds, etc.

For nonadiabatic systems of constant mass, delta U = Q + W, change in internal energy equals the heat plus the work done. This statement, which is equivalent to the law of conservation of energy, is known as the first law of thermodynamics.

A thermodynamic property of a system defined by H = U + pV, where H is the enthalpy, U is the internal energy of the system, p its pressure, and V its volume. In a chemical reaction carried out in the atmosphere the pressure remains constant and the enthalpy of reaction, delta H, is equal to delta U + p delta V. For an exothermic reaction delta H is taken to be negative.

If reactants can be converted into products by a series of reactions, the sum of the heats of these reactions (with due regard to their sign) is equal to the heat of reaction for direct conversion from reactants to products. More generally, the overall energy change in going from reactants to products does not depend on the route taken.
An amount of energy associated with a bond in a chemical compound. It is obtained from the heat of atomization.
A reversible chemical reaction in which the concentrations of reactants and products are not changing with time because the system is in thermodynamic equilibrium.

For a reversible reaction of the type xA + yB <=> zC + wD chemical equilibrium occurs when the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the back reaction, so that the concentrations of products and reactants reach steady-state values. It can be shown that at equilibrium the ratio of concentrations [C]^z[D]^w / [A]^x[B]^y is a constant for a given reaction and fixed temperature, called the equilibrium constant Kc (where the c indicates concentrations have been used).
If a system is in equilibrium, any change imposed on the system tends to shift the equilibrium to nullify the effect of the applied change.

A measure of the unavailability of a system's energy to do work; in a closed system, an increase of entropy is accompanied by a decrease in energy availability. When a system undergoes a reversible change the entropy (S) changes by an amount equal to the energy (Q) transferred to the system by heat divided by the thermodynamic temperature (T) at which this occurs; i.e. delta S = delta Q / T. However, all real processes are to a certain extent irreversible changes and in any closed system an irreversible change is always accompanied by an increase in entropy.
The pressure that each component would exert if it were present alone and occupied the same volume as the mixture of gases.

A theory, largely the work of count Rumford, James Prescott Joule, Rudolf Clausius, and James Clerk Maxwell, that explains the physical properties of matter in terms of the motions of its constituent particles. In a gas, for example, the pressure is due to the incessant impacts of the gas molecules on the walls of the container.
The flow of a gas through a small aperture. The relative rates at which gases effuse, under the same conditions, is approximately inversely proportional to the square roots of their densities.
The process by which different substances mix as a result of the random motions of their component atoms, molecules, and ions.
A gas that does not have the properties assigned to an ideal gas. Its molecules have a non-zero size and there are forces between them.

courses.name: General Chemistry Lab I
textbooks: Chemistry 103 General Chemistry Experiments, Department of Chemistry, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 2012
courses.description: Laboratory studies to accompany CHEM 102
objectives: To provide experience to the students in the performance of certain basic skills and techniques in scientific experiments.
to understand how to perform data analysis
to learn to make stop solutions and perform dilutions
to use spectroscopy and Beer’s law to determine concentration
verify a chemical composition by studying known decomposition pathways
use the ideal gas law to determine the nature of an unknown liquid substance
use calorimetry to determine the enthalpy of solution of known and unknown substances
use calorimetry to determine enthalpy of fusion and vaporization
use Hess’s law to verify enthalpy experimental data
to understand how LeChatelier’s principle works and to apply it to chemical systems
determine the equilibrium constant for a reaction experimentally
to determine Gibb’s Free energy using spectroscopy.
topics: determining the concentration of a sugar solution
determination of the molar mass of an unknown compound

d_entry: The mass of a substance per unit of volume. In SI units it is measured in kg m^-3.

The determination of the components in a chemical sample. Qualitative analysis involves determining the nature of a pure unknown compound or the compounds present in a mixture. Various chemical tests exist for different elements or types of compound, and systemic analytical procedures can be used for mixtures. Quantitative analysis involves measuring the proportions of known components in a mixture. Chemical techniques for this fall into two main classes: volumetric analysis and gravimetric analysis. In addition, there are numerous physical methods of qualitative and quantitative analysis, including spectroscopic techniques, mass spectrometry, polarography, chromatography, activation analysis, etc.

The quantity of dissolved substance per unit quantity of a solution. Concentration is measured in various ways. The amount of substance dissolved per unit volume of the solution (symbol c) has units of mol dm^-3 or mol l^-1. It is now called amount concentration (formerly molarity). The mass concentration (symbol rho) is the mass of solute per unit volume of solution. It has units of kg dm^-3, g cm^-3, etc. The molality is the amount of substance per unit mass of solvent, commonly given in units of mol kg^-1.

A law relating the reduction in luminous intensity of light passing through a material to the length of the light's path through the material: i.e. log(I/I0)=-eps[J]l, where eps is the molar absorption coefficient, I is the intensity after passing through a sample of length l, I0 is the incident intensity, and [J] is the molar concentration of species J. The Beer-Lambert law was formed empirically; however, it can be derived on the basis that the loss in intensity dI is proportional to the thickness dl of the sample, the concentration [J], and the intensity I (since the rate of absorption is proportional to the intensity).

Any of various instruments for producing a spectrum and measuring the wavelengths, energies, etc., involved. A simple type, for visible radiation, is a spectroscope equipped with a calibrated scale allowing wavelengths to be read off or calculated.
The relative proportions in which elements form compounds or in which substances react.
The amount of substance dissolved per unit volume of the solution (symbol c) has units of mol dm^-3 or mol l^-1. It is now called amount concentration (formerly molarity).

A technique for chemical analysis and the investigation of the products formed by heating a substance. In differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) heat is added to or removed from a sample electrically as the temperature is increased, this allowing the enthalpy changes due to thermal decomposition to be studied.
A change of matter in one physical phase (solid, liquid, or gas) into another. The change is invariably accompanied by the evolution or absorption of energy.

If reactants can be converted into products by a series of reactions, the sum of the heats of these reactions (with due regard to their sign) is equal to the heat of reaction for direct conversion from reactants to products. More generally, the overall energy change in going from reactants to products does not depend on the route taken.
If a system is in equilibrium, any change imposed on the system tends to shift the equilibrium to nullify the effect of the applied change.
A reversible chemical reaction in which the concentrations of reactants and products are not changing with time because the system is in thermodynamic equilibrium.

A measure of a system's ability to do work. The Gibb’s free energy (or Gibbs function), G, is defined G = H = TS where G is the energy liberated or absorbed in a reversible process at constant pressure and constant temperature (T), H is the enthalpy and S the entropy of the system. Changes in Gibbs free energy, delta G, are useful in indicating the conditions under which a chemical reaction will occur. If delta G is positive the reaction will only occur if energy is supplied to force it away from the equilibrium position (i.e. when delta G = 0). If delta G is negative the reaction will proceed spontaneously to equilibrium. The Helmholtz free energy (or Helmholtz function), F, is defined F = U - TS, where U is the internal energy. For a reversible isothermal process, delta F represents the useful work available.

courses.name: General Chemistry II
textbooks: Chemistry (on-line text); Paul Flowers, William Robinson, et.al.; OpenStax Publishing
courses.description: Chemistry 104 is a second semester course in introductory chemistry orientated for students in the biological and engineering sciences. The course covers the following topics: electrochemistry, chemical kinetics, an introduction to organic chemistry and biochemistry, and acid-base chemistry.
objectives: understand thermodynamics (first, second, and third Laws of Thermodynamics)
be able to predict spontaneity for a chemical reaction or system
be able to determine the amount of work a system could produce/needs
understand how galvanic cells work
determine the potential of a galvanic cell
understand how electrolytic cells can be used for electroplating and electrolysis
understand and apply Collision Theory to chemical reactions
be able to use differential and integrated rate laws to study chemical reactions
determine how long a reaction will take
determine what the mechanism for a chemical reaction is
be able to identify basic chemical functional groups
understand 3D shapes of basic chemical functional groups
be able to determine the absolute conformation of simple stereoisomers
predict products for and understand the mechanisms of SN1 and SN2 reactions
apply the lessons we’ve learned in previous chemistry classes as well as the material we will learn in this class to relevant real-world applications
topics: titrations of monoprotic and polyprotic acids

d_entry: A measure of the unavailability of a system's energy to do work; in a closed system, an increase of entropy is accompanied by a decrease in energy availability. When a system undergoes a reversible change the entropy (S) changes by an amount equal to the energy (Q) transferred to the system by heat divided by the thermodynamic temperature (T) at which this occurs; i.e. delta S = delta Q / T. However, all real processes are to a certain extent irreversible changes and in any closed system an irreversible change is always accompanied by an increase in entropy.

A measure of a system's ability to do work. The Gibb’s free energy (or Gibbs function), G, is defined G = H = TS where G is the energy liberated or absorbed in a reversible process at constant pressure and constant temperature (T), H is the enthalpy and S the entropy of the system. Changes in Gibbs free energy, delta G, are useful in indicating the conditions under which a chemical reaction will occur. If delta G is positive the reaction will only occur if energy is supplied to force it away from the equilibrium position (i.e. when delta G = 0). If delta G is negative the reaction will proceed spontaneously to equilibrium. The Helmholtz free energy (or Helmholtz function), F, is defined F = U - TS, where U is the internal energy. For a reversible isothermal process, delta F represents the useful work available.

A measure of a system's ability to do work. The Gibb’s free energy (or Gibbs function), G, is defined G = H = TS where G is the energy liberated or absorbed in a reversible process at constant pressure and constant temperature (T), H is the enthalpy and S the entropy of the system. Changes in Gibbs free energy, delta G, are useful in indicating the conditions under which a chemical reaction will occur. If delta G is positive the reaction will only occur if energy is supplied to force it away from the equilibrium position (i.e. when delta G = 0). If delta G is negative the reaction will proceed spontaneously to equilibrium. The Helmholtz free energy (or Helmholtz function), F, is defined F = U - TS, where U is the internal energy. For a reversible isothermal process, delta F represents the useful work available.
A device that produces an e.m.f. as a result of chemical reactions that take place within it. These reactions occur at the surface of two electrodes, each of which dips into an electrolyte.

An equation that relates the electrode potential E of an electrode that is in contact with an ionic solution to the ionic concentration c. The equation is: E = Eo - RTzF ln(c), where Eo is the standard electrode potential, R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, z is the valence of the ion, and F is the Faraday constant.
A device that produces an e.m.f. as a result of chemical reactions that take place within it. These reactions occur at the surface of two electrodes, each of which dips into an electrolyte.

The production of a chemical reaction by passing an electric current through an electrolyte. electrolysis In electrolysis, positive ions migrate to the cathode and negative ions to the anode. The reactions occurring depend on electron transfer at the electrodes and are therefore redox reactions. At the anode, negative ions in solution may lose electrons to form neutral species. Alternatively, atoms of the electrode can lose electrons and go into solution as positive ions. In either case the reaction is an oxidation. At the cathode, positive ions in solution can gain electrons to form neutral species. Thus cathode reactions are reductions.

A theory, largely the work of count Rumford, James Prescott Joule, Rudolf Clausius, and James Clerk Maxwell, that explains the physical properties of matter in terms of the motions of its constituent particles. In a gas, for example, the pressure is due to the incessant impacts of the gas molecules on the walls of the container.

The constant in an expression for the rate of a chemical reaction in terms of concentrations (or activities). For instance, in a simple unimolecular reaction A -> B, the rate is proportional to the concentration of A, i.i. rate = k[A], where k is the rate constant, which depends on the temperature. The equation is the rate equation of the reaction, and its form depends on the reaction mechanism.

In the expression for the rate of a chemical reaction, the sum of the powers of the concentrations is the overall order of the reaction. For instance, in a reaction A + B -> C, the rate equation may have the form R = k[A][B]^2. This reaction would be describes as first order in A and second order in B. The overall order is three. The order of a reaction depends on the mechanism and it is possible for the rate to be independent of concentration (zero order) or for the order to be a fraction.

An order of a chemical reaction that appears to be less than the true order because of the experimental conditions used. Pseudo orders occur when one reactant is present in large excess. For example, a reaction of substance A undergoing hydrolysis may appear to be proportional only to [A] because the amount of water present is so large.
The way in which a particular chemical reaction occurs, described in terms of the steps involved. For example, the hydrolysis of an alkyl chloride proceeds by the SN1 mechanism.

In considering the formation of molecular orbitals it is often useful to think in terms of hybrid atomic orbitals. For instance, carbon has in its outer shell one s-orbital and three p-orbitals. In forming methane (or other tetrahedral molecules) these can be regarded as combining to give four equivalent spa hybrid orbitals, each with a lobe directed to a corner of a tetrahedron. It is these that overlap with the s-orbitals on the hydrogen atoms. In ethene, two p-orbitals combine with the s-orbital to give three sp2 hybrids with lobes in a plane pointing to the corners of an equilateral triangle. These form the sigma orbitals in the C-H and C-C bonds. The remaining p-orbitals (one on each carbon) form the pi orbital. In ethyne, sp hybridization occurs to give two hybrid orbitals on each atom with lobes pointing along the axis. The two remaining p-orbitals on each carbon form two pi orbitals. Hybrid atomic orbitals can also involve d-orbitals. For instance, square-planar complexes use sped hybrids; octahedral complexes use sp3d2. Orbital hybridization can be discussed using group theory.
The branch of chemistry concerned with compounds of carbon.

Prefix used before the name of an element to indicate compounds of the elements containing organic groups (with the element bound to carbon atoms). For example, lead(IV)tetraethyl is an organolead compound.

Saturated hydrocarbons with the general formula CnH2n+2. In systematic chemical nomenclature alkane names end in the suffix -ane. They form a homologous series (the alkane series) methane (CH4), ethane (C2H6), propane (C3H8), butane (C4H10), pentane (C5H12) etc. The lower members of the series are gases; the high-molecular weight alkanes are waxy solids. Alkanes are present in natural gas and petroleum.
The determination or estimation of the relative energies of possible conformations of a molecule and the effect of these on the molecule's properties.

A type of substitution reaction in which a nucleophile displaces another group or atom from a compound. For example in CR3Cl + OH- -> CR3OH + Cl- the nucleophile is the OH- ion. There are two possible mechanisms of nucleophilic substitution. In SN1 reactions, a positive carbonium ion is first formed: CR3Cl -> CR3+ + Cl-. This then reacts with the nucleophile Cr3+ + OH- -> CR3OH. The CR3+ ion is planar and the OH- ion can attack from either side. Consequently, if the original molecule is optically active (the three R groups are different) then a racemic mixture of products results. The alternative mechanism, the SN2 reaction, is a concerted reaction in which the nucleophile approaches from the side of the R groups as the other group (Cl in the example) leaves. In this case the configuration of the molecule is inverted. If the original molecule is optically active, the product has the opposite activity, an effect known as Walden inversion. The notations SN1 and SN2 refer to the kinetics of the reactions. In the SN1 mechanism, the slow step is the first one, which is unimolecular (and first order in CR3Cl). In the SN2 reaction, the process is bimolecular (and second order overall).

A way of denoting the absolute structure of an optical isomer. The R-S convention is a convention based on priority of groups attached to the chiral carbon atom. The order priority is I, Br, Cl, SO3H, OCOCH3, OH, NO2, COOCH3, CONH2, COCH3, CHO, CH2OH, C6H5, C2H5, CH3, H, with hydrogen lowest. The molecule is viewed with the group of lowest priority behind the chiral atom.

A system for the unambiguous description of stereoisomers used in the R-S convention and in the E-Z convention. The system involves a sequence rule for determining a conventional order of ligands. The rule is that the atom bonded directly to the chiral centre or double bond is considered and the ligand in which this atom has the highest proton number takes precedence. So, for example, I takes precedence over Cl. If two ligands have bonding atoms with the same proton number, then substituents are taken into account (with the substituent of highest proton number taking precedence). Thus, -C2H5 has a higher precedence than -CH3. If a double (or triple) bond occurs to a substituent, then the substituent is counted twice (or three times). An isotope of high nucleon number takes precedence over one of lower nucleon number. Hydrogen always has lowest priority in this system. For example, the sequence for some common ligands is I, Br, CI, SO3H, OCOCH3, OCH3, OH, NO2, NH2, COOCH3, CONH2, COCH3, CHO, CH2OH, C6115, C2H5, CH3, H. The system was jointly developed in 1966 by the British chemists Robert Sidney Cahn (1899-1981) and Sir Christopher Kelk Ingold (1893-1970) and the Bosnian-Swiss chemist Vladimir Prelog (1906-98).

A type of compound that contains hydrogen and dissociates in water to produce positive hydrogen ions. The reaction, for an acid HX, is commonly written HX <=> H+ + X-. In fact, the hydrogen ion (the proton) is solvated, and the complete reaction is: HX + H2O <=> H3O+ + X-. The ion H3O+ is the oxonium ion (or hydroxonium ion or hydronium ion). This definition of acids comes from the Arrhenius theory. Such acids tend to be corrosive substances with sharp taste, which turns litmus red and give colour changes with other indicators.

A logarithmic scale for expressing the acidity or alkalinity of a solution. To a first approximation, the pH of a solution can be defined as -log c, where c is the concentration of hydrogen ions in moles per cubic decimetre. A neutral solution at 25°C has a hydrogen-ion concentration of 10-7 mol dm-3, so the pH is 7. A pH below 7 indicates an acid solution; one above 7 indicates an alkaline solution. More accurately, the pH depends not on the concentration of hydrogen ions but on their •act. , which cannot be measured experimentally. For practical purposes, the pH scale is defined by using a hydrogen electrode in the solution of interest as one half of a cell, with a reference electrode (e.g. a calomel electrode) as the other half cell. The pH is then given by (E- ER)F/2.303RT, where E is the e.m.f. of the cell and ER the standard electrode potential of the reference electrode, and F the Faraday constant. In practice, a glass electrode is more convenient than a hydrogen electrode. pH stands for 'potential of hydrogen'. The scale was introduced by Soren Sorensen (1868-1939) in 1909.
An acid that is only partially dissociated in aqueous solution.

An ion that is common to two or more components in a mixture. In a solution of XCl, for example, the addition of another chloride YCl, may precipitate XCl because the solubility product is exceeded as a result of the extra concentration of Cl- ions.

A method of volumetric analysis in which a volume of one reagent (the titrant) is added to a known volume of another reagent slowly from a burette until an end point is reached. The volume added before the end point is reached is noted. If one of the solutions has a known concentration, that of the other can be calculated.
A polymer that occurs naturally, such as a polysaccharide, protein, or nucleic acid.

A protein that acts as a catalyst in biochemical reactions. Each enzyme is specific to a particular reaction or group of similar reactions. Many require the association of certain nonprotein cofactors  in order to function. The molecule undergoing reaction (the substrate) binds to a specific active site  on the enzyme molecule to form a short-lived intermediate: this greatly increases (by a factor of up to 1020) the rate at which the reaction proceeds to form the product. Enzyme activity is influenced by substrate concentration and by temperature and pH, which must lie within a certain range. Other molecules may compete for the active site, causing inhibition of the enzyme or even irreversible destruction of its catalytic properties. Enzyme production is governed by a cell's genes. Enzyme activity is further controlled by pH changes, alterations in the concentrations of essential cofactors, feedback inhibition by the products of the reaction, and activation by another enzyme, either from a less active form or an inactive precursor (zymogen). Such changes may themselves be under the control of hormones or the nervous system.

courses.name: Intro to Computing: Engrg & Sci
textbooks: A Primer on Scientific Programming with Python, 5th edition, Hans Petter Langtangen, ISBN: 978-3-662-49886-6
courses.description: Fundamental principles, concepts, and methods of computing, with emphasis on applications in the physical sciences and engineering. Basic problem solving and programming techniques; fundamental algorithms and data structures; use of computers in solving engineering and scientific problems. Intended for engineering and science majors.
objectives: Goal 1 Students should be able to solve problems algorithmically. Goal 2 Students should be proficient in 'computational thinking', meaning that students should be able to conduct major- appropriate abstraction, modeling, representations of information, and algorithmic thinking related to solving engineering problems. Goal 3 Students should be confident about using computation as a standard tool, on the same level as math and physics. Goal 4 Students should be able to program in a high-level language (e.g., Python or MATLAB). Goal 5 Students should access data sources, process data, and create outputs including various, plots. Goal 6 Students should use MATLAB proficiently.

courses.name: Electrical and Electronic Ckts
textbooks: Analog Signals and Systems, Erhan Kudeki and David C. Munson Jr.
courses.description: ECE 205 is an introductory course on circuit analysis and electronics for non-majors in engineering. The course includes bi-weekly electronics lab experiments designed to provide students with hands-on experience. Basic principles of circuit analysis and DC circuits; time-domain analysis of 1st and 2nd order linear circuits; complex numbers, phasors, AC steady-state analysis; frequency response; op-amp, diode, and BJT circuits; logic gates and digital logic circuits. Credit is not given to Computer or Electrical Engineering majors.
objectives: ECE 205 is an introductory course on circuit analysis and electronics for non-majors in engineering. The goals are to impart the fundamental principles of electric circuits and electronic circuits that constitute the foundation for preparing a non-major to take follow-on courses involving electric and electronic circuits.
topics: Step response with sinusoidal forcing function
Transient response to second order circuits

courses.name: Electrical and Electronics Lab
courses.description: Laboratory experiments in digital logic and controllers; transistor amplifier and switching circuits; DC motor control and voltage regulators; sensors and motion control with feedback; wireless communication.
objectives: ECE206 is the lab course which builds on ECE 205 concepts; ECE 205 is an introductory course in circuit analysis for non-majors in engineering. The goals are to supplement the material of ECE 205 and provide a hands-on experience in assembling and testing electric and electronic circuits.
topics: Measure propagation delay, noise margin, power dissipation
Driving a load with an H-Bridge

courses.name: Calculus I
textbooks: Stewart, Calculus: Early Transcendentals, 7th edition with Enhanced WebAssign, Cengage Publishing
courses.description: First course in calculus and analytic geometry for students with some calculus background; basic techniques of differentiation and integration with applications including curve sketching; antidifferentation, the Riemann integral, fundamental theorem, exponential and trigonometric functions.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus
topics: Rates of Change in the Natural and Social Sciences

courses.name: Calculus II
textbooks: Stewart, Calculus: Early Transcendentals, 7th edition with Enhanced WebAssign, Cengage Publishing
courses.description: Second course in calculus and analytic geometry: techniques of integration, conic sections, polar coordinates, and infinite series.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus
topics: Integration of Rational Functions by Partial Fractions
Area of a Surface of Revolution
Representation of Functions by Power Series
Areas and Lengths in Polar Coordinates

courses.name: Calculus III
textbooks: Stewart, Calculus: Early Transcendentals, 7th edition with Enhanced WebAssign, Cengage Publishing
courses.description: Third course in calculus and analytic geometry including vector analysis: Euclidean space, partial differentiation, multiple integrals, line integrals and surface integrals, the integral theorems of vector calculus.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus
topics: Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals
The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals

courses.name: Intro to Differential Equations
textbooks: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations and Boundary Value Problems: 10th edition.
courses.description: Techniques and applications of ordinary differential equations, including Fourier series and boundary value problems, linear systems of differential equations, and an introduction to partial differential equations. Covers all the topics of MATH 285 plus linear systems. Intended for engineering majors and other who require a working knowledge of differential equations.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of differential equations to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using differential equations
topics: Differences Between Linear and Nonlinear Equations
Complex Roots of the Characteristic Equations
General Theory of nth Order Linear Equations
The Occurrence of Two-Point Boundary Value Problems

courses.name: Applied Linear Algebra
textbooks: Gilbert Strang, Linear Algebra and Its Applications, 4th Edition, Cengage Publishing
courses.description: Introductory course emphasizing techniques of linear algebra with applications to engineering; topics include matrix operations, determinants, linear equations, vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors, inner products and norms, orthogonality, equilibrium, and linear dynamical systems.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of linear algebra to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using linear algebra

courses.name: University Physics, Mechanics
textbooks: Gladding, Selen & Stelzer, Classical Mechanics, W.H. Freeman (1st ed)
courses.description: Newton's Laws, work and energy, static properties and dynamics, fluids, oscillations, transverse waves, systems of particles, and rotations. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of mechanics
apply mechanics concepts to solve a wide array of relevant problems.
interactive lectures to present, discuss, and demonstrate the principles
laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles
online homework to engage students in problem solving
interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem-solving skills.
topics: center of mass reference frame, impulse
angular momentum about a fixed axis
angular momentum of a freely moving particle

courses.name: University Physics: Elec & Mag
textbooks: Gladding, Selen & Stelzer, Electricity & Magnetism, W.H. Freeman (1st ed.)
FlipItPhysics online activities
courses.description: Coulomb's Law, electric fields, Gauss' Law, electric potential, capacitance, circuits, magnetic forces and fields, Ampere's law, induction, electromagnetic waves, polarization, and geometrical optics. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: learn the basic concepts of electricity and magnetism
students view prelectures and answer checkpoint questions that are used to prepare lectures
interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles
laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles
online homework to engage students in problem solving
interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills
topics: potential energy due to B field
relationship between E and B in an e-m wave

courses.name: Univ Physics: Thermal Physics
textbooks: Wolfe, Elements of Thermal Physics, 3rd edition.
courses.description: First and second laws of thermodynamics including kinetic theory of gases, heat capacity, heat engines, introduction to entropy and statistical mechanics, and introduction to application of free energy and Boltzmann factor. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics and chemistry
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of thermal physics and apply them to solve a wide array of relevant problems. To achieve these goals several reinforcing activities are used: (1) interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles; (2) laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles; (3) online homework to engage students in problem solving; (4) interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills.

courses.name: Univ Physics: Quantum Physics
textbooks: Young & Freedman, University Physics with Modern Physics, Custom Edition (12th ed)
courses.description: Interference and diffraction, photons and matter waves, the Bohr atom, uncertainty principle, and wave mechanics. A calculus-based course for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of waves and quantum physics and apply them to solve a wide array of relevant problems. To achieve these goals several reinforcing activities are used: (1) interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles; (2) laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles; (3) online homework to engage students in problem solving; (4) interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills.

courses.name: Statistics and Probability I
textbooks: Robert V. Hogg and Elliot A. Tanis, Probability and Statistical Inference, 8th Edition, Prentice Hall, 2010.
courses.description: Introduction to mathematical statistics that develops probability as needed; includes the calculus of probability, random variables, expectation, distribution functions, central limit theorem, point estimation, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Offers a basic one-term introduction to statistics and also prepares students for STAT 410.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of probability and statistics to problems in science and engineering
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using probability and statistics
topics: Tests of the Equality of Two Means

courses.name: Introduction to Statics
textbooks: Beer and Johnston, Vector Mechanics for Engineers: Statics, 10th edition, McGraw-Hill.
courses.description: Forces, moments, and couples; resultants of force systems; equilibrium analysis and free-body diagrams; analysis of forces acting on members of trusses, frames, etc.; shear-force and bending-moment distributions; Coulomb friction; centroids and center of mass; applications of statics in design.
objectives: 1. Be able to calculate resultants of discrete and distributed force systems. [1,2] 2. Be able to draw correct free body diagrams. [1,2] 3. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems for particles, systems of particles, rigid bodies, frames, and machines. [1,2] 4. Be able to analyze trusses by the methods of joints and of sections. [1,2] 5. Be able to calculate internal forces and moments in frame and machine members. [1,2] 6. Be able to draw shear-force and bending-moment diagrams. [1,2] 7. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving Coulomb friction. [1,2] 8. Be able to calculate centroids, mass centers, and centers of gravity of general bodies. [1,2]

courses.name: Statics
textbooks: Beer and Johnston, Vector Mechanics for Engineers: Statics, 10th edition, McGraw-Hill.
courses.description: Forces, moments, and couples; resultants of force systems; equilibrium analysis and free-body diagrams; analysis of forces acting on members of trusses, frames, etc.; shear-force and bending-moment distributions; Coulomb friction; centroids, center of mass, moment of inertia, polar moment of inertia, and product of inertia; virtual work; hydrostatic pressure; applications of statics in design.
objectives: 1. Be able to calculate resultants of discrete and distributed force systems. [1,2] 2. Be able to draw correct free body diagrams. [1,2] 3. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems for particles, systems of particles, rigid bodies, frames, and machines. [1,2] 4. Be able to analyze trusses by the methods of joints and of sections. [1,2] 5. Be able to calculate internal forces and moments in frame and machine members. [1,2] 6. Be able to draw shear-force and bending-moment diagrams. [1,2] 7. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving Coulomb friction. [1,2] 8. Be able to calculate centroids, mass centers, and centers of gravity of general bodies. [1,2] 9. Be able to calculate moments and products of inertia of plane regions. [1,2] 10. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems in terms of the principle of virtual work. [1,2] 118 11. Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving hydrostatic pressure. [1,2]
topics: Trusses, methods of joint and sections

courses.name: Introductory Dynamics
textbooks: McGill, D. J., and King, W. W., An Introduction to Dynamics, 4th edition, Tichenor Publishing.
courses.description: Kinematics and dynamics of the three-dimensional motion of particles; kinematics and dynamics of the plane motion of rigid bodies; methods of work energy and impulse momentum; moving reference frames.
objectives: 1. Be able to draw free-body diagrams, especially for moving bodies. [1] 2. Be able to calculate centers of mass and moments and products of inertia of solid bodies. [1] 3. Be able to determine analytically the (Cartesian coordinate) trajectory of particles and mass centers subjected to specified time-, velocity-, and position-dependent forces. [1] 4. Be able to calculate the vector velocity and acceleration of a particle whose motion is described in cylindrical, polar, Cartesian, or path variables. [1] 5. Be able to set up and solve for the forces acting on an accelerating particle or rigid body. [1,2] 6. Be able to identify and use the principles of work-energy and energy conservation to calculate the motion of particles and rigid bodies. [1,2] 7. Be able to identify and use the principles of (angular) impulse-momentum and (angular) momentum conservation to calculate the motion of particles and rigid bodies. [1,2] 8. Be able to analyze collisions, and determine subsequent motion, using principles of momentum and angular momentum conservation. [1,2] 9. Be able to apply the relationships between the velocities and accelerations of points of a rigid body in plane motion. [1] 10. Be able to apply the relations between the velocities and accelerations of points that may be moving relative to a rigid body in plane motion. [1]
topics: Kinematics of 2D motion of rigid bodies
Dynamics of 2D motion of rigid bodies

courses.name: Introductory Solid Mechanics
textbooks: Hibbeler,R.C., Mechanics of Materials, 10th edition, Pearson
courses.description: on deformable bodies, and design principles based on mechanics of solids: normal stresses, shear stresses, and deformations produced by tensile, compressive, torsional, and bending loading of members; beam deflections; elastic energy and impact; multi-dimensional stress states; and buckling of columns.
objectives: 1. Be able to calculate normal stresses in straight bars subjected to combinations of bending and either tension or compression. [1,2] 2. Be able to apply principles of failure analysis and factors of safety based on stress. [1,2] 3. Be able to calculate shear stresses in transversely loaded beams and in torsionally loaded members of circular cross section. [1,2] 4. Be able to calculate maximum principal stresses and principal stress orientations in combined loading cases of generalized plane stress. [1,2] 5. Be able to calculate deflections of elastic beams subjected to simple types of transverse loading. [1,2] 6. Be able to determine the buckling loads of slender columns with simple types of end condition. [1,2]
topics: Basic concepts of stress and strain
design of shafts for power transmission
design of beams for structural applications
slopes, shear forces, and bending moments
states of plane stress and plane strain
design problems for combined states of stress

courses.name: Intermediate Dynamics
textbooks: L. Meirovitch., 2001, Fundamentals of Vibrations, Waveland Press
courses.description: Lagrangian mechanics of dynamical systems with an emphasis on vibrations; constraints and generalized coordinates; motion in accelerating frames; conservation laws and invariance of the Lagrangian; particle motion in one dimension, the two-body problem, and central-force motion; free and forced vibration of linearized single-degree-of-freedom and multi-degree-of-freedom discrete systems; weakly nonlinear vibrations; parametric resonance; introduction to Hamiltonian dynamics; rigid-body motions.
objectives: 1. Be able to identify suitable generalized coordinates for configuration-constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies. [1] 2. Be able to express the kinetic energy, potential energy and Lagrangian of a system of particles and rigid bodies in terms of the chosen generalized coordinates and corresponding generalized velocities and the inertial properties for each body (which may require additional computations to determine). [1] 3. Be able to write down appropriate configuration constraint equations. [1] 4. Be able to deduce Lagrange’s equations of motion from the Lagrangian treating the applicable constraints appropriately, possibly through the introduction of Lagrange multipliers. [1] 5. Be able to numerically integrate the equations of motion for configuration-constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies. [1] 124 6. Be able to set up the equations of motion for small, free and forced, oscillations about an equilibrium for both single- and multi-degree-of-freedom systems, and calculate the linearized characteristic frequencies and normal modes of free oscillations. [1] 7. Be able to identify ignorable coordinates and their associated integrals of the motion. [1] 8. Be able to study forced resonances of single- and multi-degree-of-freedom systems and design for vibration isolation. [1] 9. Be able to analytically and/or numerically compute the free and forced response of single-and multi-degree-of-freedom oscillators subject to general transient or periodic forces. [1]
topics: Generalized coordinates and degrees of freedom
unconstrained and constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies
Potential energy and relation to generalized forces
Ignorable coordinates and conservation laws of Lagrangian mechanics
Small oscillations about positions of equilibrium
Matrix representations of equations of motion
Eigenvalue and eigenvector decompositions of symmetric matrices
Elimination of ignorable coordinates using Routhians
Derivation of Hamilton’s principle of least action

courses.name: Continuum Mechanics
textbooks: Nair, Introduction to Continuum Mechanics, Cambridge University Press.
courses.description: Tensor algebra and analysis; kinematics of continua; mass, force, stress, and the general balance laws of continuum mechanics; introduction to constitutive equations.
objectives: 1. Be able to calculate with tensors from both the component and direct points of view. [1] 2. Be able to calculate velocity and acceleration fields of deforming continua from both the material/referential and spatial points of view. [1] 3. Be able to state and apply the basic balance laws of conservation of mass, balance of momentum, and balance of moment of momentum in both local and global forms. [1] 4. Be able to state constitutive equations from some of the simpler theories, such as Newtonian fluids, and apply them in the formulation of boundary/initial-value problems. [1]
topics: principal directions of symmetric second-order tensors
some properties of the stress tensor

courses.name: Computational Mechanics
textbooks: Moin, P., Fundamentals of Engineering Numerical Analysis, Second Edition, Cambridge University Press (2010).
courses.description: Modern computational mechanics: mappings and iterative methods; stability; convergence; consistency; numerical and symbolic solutions of ordinary and partial differential equations; finite-difference methods; the finite-element method; spectral methods. Applications to problems in solid mechanics, fluid mechanics, and dynamics.
objectives: 1. Be able to select and apply numerical techniques appropriate for numerical approxi-mation of ordinary and partial differential equations that arise in mechanics. [1] 2. Be able to analyze numerical techniques for accuracy, consistency, and stability. [1] 3. Be able to devise and apply techniques for a posteriori estimation of numerical truncation errors. [1] 4. Be able to design appropriate validation and verification procedures for a computational mechanics simulation in the context of the ethical responsibilities of the numerical analysis in engineering practice. [1,4,7]
topics: the nature of computational solid and fluid mechanics
Ordinary differential equations arising in mechanics
Examples from solid and fluid mechanics
systems with a finite number of modes
Partial differential equations arising in mechanics
discussion of the simplest advection equation
physical interpretation of consistency and stability
Navier-Stokes equation in a periodic box (2D and 3D)
split-step method for nonlinear Schrödinger equation

courses.name: Computer Aided Design
textbooks: Smith, S. G., Pro/Engineer Wildfire 5.0 Basic Design, CAD Quest Inc.
courses.description: Geometry and topology of engineered components: creation of engineering models and their presentation in standard 2d blueprint form and as 3D wire-frame and shaded solids; meshed topologies for engineering analysis and tool-path generation for component manufacture; ISO and ANSI standards for coordinate dimensioning and tolerancing, with modeling software for creating associative models at the component and assembly levels with automatic blueprint creation, interference checking, and linked bill of materials.
objectives: 1. Be able to create fully defined designs of engineered components to ANSI and ISO standards. [1,2] 2. Be able to present component design and form specifications in standard 2D blueprint form (orthographic views), 3D wireframe and shaded solid models. [1,2] 3. Be able to create assembly models, exploded views, linked Bill of Materials (BOM), full view and sectioned orthographic assembly drawings. [1,2] 4. Be able to develop a functional mechanical design with appreciation of limits and fits required for interchangeable assembly, functionality of fits (bearing fits, press and push fits, etc.), and economics of manufacture. [1,2] 5. Be able to develop a product design specification for a new product concept addressing broad issues of societal impact, safety, environmental, economic and ethical considerations. [2,4,5] 6. Be able to professionally present design concepts in written and oral form. [2,4,5]
topics: visualization (hidden lines, shaded, and perspective views)
coordinate dimensioning and tolerancing principles and standards

courses.name: Thermodynamics
textbooks: Moran, M. J. and H.N. Shapiro, Fundamentals of Engineering Thermodynamics, 7th edition, John Wiley and Sons.
courses.description: Introduction to classical thermodynamics through the second law; system and control volume analyses of thermodynamic processes; irreversibility and availability; relations for ideal gas mixtures.
objectives: 2. An ability to determine properties and thermodynamic states. [1,2,4,7] 3. An ability to analyze mass movements and energy flow/conversions. [1,2,4,6,7] 4. An ability to understand thermodynamic performance limits of cycles and devices. [1,2,4,7] 5. An ability to determine and understand sources of irreversibility, and methods for reducing irreversibilities. [1,2,4] 6. An ability to apply thermodynamic analysis tools to understanding and evaluating emerging power cycles and devices against their traditional counterparts. [1,2,3,4]
topics: First Law of Thermodynamics for a control mass (closed system)
First law of thermodynamics for a control volume (open system)
examples of power and refrigeration cycles
Properties and thermodynamics of ideal gas mixtures

courses.name: Design for Manufacturability
textbooks: Groover, M.P., Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing, 5th edition, Wiley
courses.description: Introduction to DFM methodologies and tools; material selection (new and traditional materials); designing for primary manufacturing processes (cutting fundamentals, casting, forming, and shaping); designing with plastics (snap-fits, integral hinges, etc.); design for assembly (DFA); geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (GD&T).
objectives: 1. Ability to evaluate trade-offs between alternative design and process selection decisions with regard to manufacturability. [1,2,4] 2. Broad working knowledge of manufacturing processes and technologies. [1,2,4] 3. Ability to work as a team on a design project and consider in detail a broad spectrum of issues including manufacturability, cost (local and global), environmental, safety, ethical, political, and cultural context. [1,2,3,4,5] 4. Ability to implement DFM methodologies and understanding of fundamental issues in CAD/ CAM, metrology, and manufacturing automation. [1,2,6] 5. Ability to quantitatively estimate forces, powers, pressures, and rates in metal forming and material removal processes, and choose appropriate operating parameters. [1,2,6] 6. Ability to design and conduct laboratory experiments with manufacturing processes and metrology equipment. Familiarity with CAD/CAM and process simulation software as a tool for design and process planning tasks. [1,2,6] 7. Broad knowledge of manufacturing processes and technologies that are currently in the developmental stage. [4]

courses.name: Fundamentals of Fluid Dynamics
textbooks: Munson, Young, Okiishi, Huebsch, Fundamentals of Fluid Mechanics, 7th edition, John Wiley and Sons
courses.description: Introduction to fluid mechanics with coverage of theory and applications of incompressible viscous and inviscid flows, and compressible high-speed flows.
objectives: 1. Be able to analyze hydrostatic pressures, forces, and moments. [1,2] 2. Be able to apply control volume and/or differential analyses to incompressible viscous and inviscid flow, and high-speed compressible flow. [1,2] 3. Have a working knowledge of fluid flow devices. [1,2] 4. Be able to design, conduct, analyze, and present the results from fluid flow experiments. [1,2,3,4,5,6]
topics: Hydrostatics, including nanometers, Bourdon gauges and pressure transducers

courses.name: Heat Transfer
textbooks: Bergman, T.L., Lavine A.S., Incropera, F. P., and DeWitt, D.P., Fundamentals of Heat and Mass Transfer, 7th edition, John Wiley and Sons.
courses.description: Principles and application of heat transfer by conduction, convection, and thermal radiation.
objectives: 1. Be able to analyze temperature distributions, heat fluxes and heat transfer rates in conduction heat transfer. [1,2,5] 2. Be able to analyze flow and convective heat transfer rates in simple geometries. [1,2,5] 3. Be able to determine heat transfer rates by radiation. [1,2,5] 4. Have a working knowledge of temperature measurement devices. [1,2,5] 5. Be able to analyze and document methodology and results of laboratory experiments. [3,5,6] 6. Be able to design thermal equipment (e.g., heat exchangers) considering performance, cost, and durability. [4,5]
topics: Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction, heat transfer

courses.name: Engineering Materials
textbooks: Callister, W. D., Materials Science and Engineering: An Introduction, 8th edition, John Wiley and Sons.
courses.description: Structures of polymers, metals, and ceramics as the basis for their mechanical behavior. Manipulation of structure through processes such as heat treatment and solidification. Mechanisms of material failure in service (yielding, fracture, fatigue, creep, corrosion, wear) and simple design techniques to avoid these failures. Strategies for material selection in design.
objectives: 1. Be able to converse intelligently with materials experts. [1,3,5,6] 2. Be aware of the major failure mechanisms of materials in service and the basic design methodologies associated with them. [1,6] 3. Be able to choose an appropriate material for a given application, based on a working knowledge of how different materials behave (properties) and why (microstructure). [1,6] 4. Be able to make basic design decisions regarding materials processes in order to produce the microstructure necessary to achieve a desired set of material properties. [1,6] 5. Be able to conduct, analyze, and present the results from experiments with materials. [1,3,5,6] 6. Be able to use the latest software to enter and collect experimental data. [1,3,5,6] 7. Be able to write a good engineering report. [3] 8. Be able to create and deliver a good engineering presentation. [3]
topics: wire drawing, rolling, forging, annealing, recrystallization, grain growth
solution, quenching and heat transfer, aging
casting, solidification time, stresses of solidification
matrix materials and the role of the matrix
particulate, slip casting, sintering, glass processing

courses.name: Dynamics of Mechanical Systems
textbooks: Esfandiari, R. and Lu. B, Modeling and Analysis of Dynamic Systems, 1st edition, Taylor andFrancis.
courses.description: Dynamic modeling of mechanical components and systems; time domain and frequency domain analysis of linear time invariant systems; multi-degree-of-freedom systems; linearization of nonlinear systems.
objectives: 1. Be able to analyze various types of dynamic systems: mechanical, electrical, etc. [1] 2. Be able to coordinate analytical concepts with experimental data seen on dynamic systems. Also relate analytical concepts to everyday systems. [6,1] 3. Utilize systems concepts to understand complicated systems from simpler subsystems. [1]
topics: solutions of differential equations by Laplace transform
Time domain analysis of linear time invariant systems
applications to beat generation and vibration absorbers

courses.name: Signal Processing
textbooks: Ambardar, A., Analog and Digital Signal Processing, 2nd edition, Brooks/Cole Publishing.
Åström, Karl J. and Richard M. Murray, Feedback Systems: An Introduction for Scientists and Engineers, 2012 revision, http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/amwiki/Main_Page.
Joseph Bentsman, Introduction to Signal Processing, Instrumentation, and Control: an Integrative Approach, World Scientific Publishing, 2016.
courses.description: of transducers and actuators. Signal conditioning, grounding, and shielding. Analog and digital signal processing and feedback control methods with emphasis on frequency domain techniques. Frequency response of continuous and discrete systems.
objectives: 1. Understand the operating principle and the application context of basic transducers. [1,6,7] 2. Understand the operating principle and the application context of basic actuators. [1,6,7] 3. Be able to understand, specify, and use data collection systems. [1,6,7] 4. Be able to analyze frequency domain properties of the collected data and relate it to physical time domain phenomena in the system. [1,6,7] 5. Be able to understand, specify, and use industrial controllers. [1,6,7]
topics: continuous time systems and Laplace transforms
Impulse response and convolution for continuous time systems
Sampling theory and introduction to aliasing
Instrumentation amplifiers and transducer signal conditioning
Modeling and identification of electric motor using step response
Speed control of a DC electric motor
PID control of a hydraulic cylinder

courses.name: Mechanical Design I
textbooks: Norton, R. L., Design of Machinery with Working Model Software, 5rd edition, McGraw-Hill.
courses.description: Kinematics and dynamics of machinery, including introduction to user-centered design and design thinking, analytical and computer-aided design of kinematics, dynamic force analysis, principle of virtual work, cam and gear design, and balancing. Project-based learning of multi-mechanism system design, analysis, fabrication, and evaluation.
objectives: 1. Understand mechanical machines as assemblies of kinematic mechanisms. [1,2] 2. Perform position, velocity, acceleration, dynamic force, and balancing analyses on planar mechanisms. [1,2] 3. Design planar mechanisms that execute desired motions with acceptable dynamic forces. [1,2] 4. Understand how truss and frame structures carry static loads, via tension, compression, bending, and torsion. [1,2] 5. Design simple truss and frame structures that have adequate load-carrying ability and stiffness. [1,2,7] 6. Analyze truss and frame structures for stress and deflection, using finite element methods. [1,2] 7. Present design and analysis of mechanical machines or structures in written and/or oral reports. [1,2,3,5]
topics: stress, strain, elastic modulus, yield stress
shear force, bending, moment, strain curvature
Matrix/finite element method for assemblies of springs and frame structures
MATLAB program kinematics and dynamic force analysis
design process such as empathy, ideation methods
Design review labs to present and discuss project

courses.name: Mechanical Design II
textbooks: Juvinall and Marshek, Fundamentals of Machine Component Design, 5th edition or latest, Wiley.
courses.description: material failure modes, including yielding, fracture, and fatigue. Design and selection of machine elements: threaded fasteners, springs, rolling-element bearings, fluid film lubrication, gears and friction drives.
objectives: 1. Ability to utilize computer-aided design and engineering tools in conducting linear stress analysis. [1,2] 2. Analyze structures for various failure modes (yielding, fracture, fatigue, and creep) to predict failure loads and service lifetimes. [1,2] 3. Design structures that can withstand service loads and have adequate lifetimes. [1,2,3,5] 4. Be familiar with common structural materials and their properties. [1,2] 5. Design and/or select springs for desired stiffness, energy absorption, and lifetime. [1,2] 6. Analyze bolted and welded joints for load-carrying ability and service lifetime. [1,2] 7. Design joints that can withstand service loads and have adequate lifetimes. [1,2,3,5] 8. Analyze power transmission components (gears, clutches, brakes, bearings) and systems for kinematics, service lifetimes, and supporting loads. [1,2] 9. Select and size power transmission components to achieve suitable kinematics, service lifetime, and transmission of power. [1,2,3,5]
topics: Use CAE to evaluate the strength of components
Demonstrate the strengths and weaknesses of elastic FEA
Use beams, solid and shell elements to estimate component strength
